summary.overall: Chapter 7 was reviewed in 18 bounded source-grounded shards. Proposed changes: 246 replacements, 3 splits, and 15 removals.
patterns: Paragraph-location scaffolding is universal across questions and activities: stems lead with 'Under X-X-X' and explanations begin with 'Per X-X-X,' teaching document navigation instead of operational knowledge.

Answer-length cues in two multiple-choice questions where the correct answer enumerates source conditions while distractors are short and generic, making the answer identifiable without domain knowledge.
One true/false question tests whether published phraseology is 'associated with' a paragraph rather than testing when and how to apply the phraseology operationally.
Explanations in three activities include paragraph-location citations that add no instructional value.
Four of five flashcards are well-formed with clean fronts and concise backs; the fifth needs only scaffolding removal.
No source-fidelity errors, no duplicate concepts across formats, and no unsafe operational claims.
Paragraph-location scaffolding is the dominant defect, affecting all 12 questions, 6 of 8 activities, and 1 flashcard. Stems and explanations routinely embed 'Under 7-X-Y' or 'Per 7-X-Y' prefixes.
Activity explanations in 7-4-1 are reduced to paragraph pointers ('That definition appears in the opening sentence of 7-4-1') with zero instructional value.

The spot_the_error activities are educationally sound — they present a specific phraseology error for identification and correction — but their explanations lean on paragraph references rather than the operational reason for the correct wording.
True/false items in this shard do not suffer from negative-stem defects despite automated flagging; the flags are false positives for declarative T/F stems.
Flashcard e5b79368 (7-3-1) contains both a source-fidelity error and overloaded retrieval targets across pilot duties and ATC responsibilities that belong in separate cards.
Question 22ef87da (7-4-1) exhibits a clear answer-length cue: the correct definition is notably longer than the distractors.

Pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: 14 of 19 items embed paragraph references ('Under 7-4-X', 'Per 7-4-X') in stems, explanations, or para_context fields, teaching document structure instead of operational substance.
Explanations that restate answers: the original explanation on cd15393a simply echoes the correct choice without teaching when, why, or how the rule applies.

Correct content in wrong wrapper: several items have sound questions and distractors but are embedded in stems that cite paragraph numbers, making the review scope predominantly cosmetic removal of scaffolding.
Unflagged activities are clean: the two activities without automated flags (37ac9ce1, 8cbfa20d) are well-formed with self-contained stems, plausible distractors, and adequate explanations.
Universal paragraph-location scaffolding in all 9 quiz question stems; all 9 required replacement with self-contained stems.
Explanations throughout the shard were factually accurate and taught the controlling rule without invented safety rationales.

Activities across paragraphs were of good quality — situation_action scenarios placed the learner in realistic operational contexts, spot_the_error items targeted correct vs. incorrect phraseology tokens, and the SVFR readback_check was an appropriate use of that activity type.
Flashcards were concise, self-contained, and tested operational recall without paragraph-number references.
Some activities carried minor paragraph-number references in para_context and explanation fields, but these were supplementary and did not undermine the operational substance of the item.

Paragraph-location scaffolding is the dominant defect — 13 items begin stems with 'Under 7-5-X' or explanations with 'Per 7-5-X.' These references test which paragraph a rule lives in, not whether the controller can apply it.
Answer-length cue in question 0b752578: the correct answer is three words while every distractor is 10–14 words.
Activity 26624a85 omits the MSA floor ('not below the minimum safe altitude prescribed in 14 CFR 91.119') from the correct answer. The source explicitly includes this constraint.
Explanations largely restate the answer rather than teaching the controlling principle, condition, or exception. Remediated explanations expand on the operational rule.
The three flashcards are concise, accurate, and appropriately scoped to single retrieval targets. No intervention needed.

Negative-stem automated flags (ac65ca43, 6092f483, d9c7c634) are false positives: each negative formulation tests exclusion recognition or the reason for a prohibition, which is the actual operational skill.

Uniform paragraph-location scaffolding in stems and explanations — all questions start with 'Under 7-5-X' and explanations with 'Per 7-5-X'; activity para_context and explanations follow the same pattern.
Answer-length cue in 6a52ac29 where the correct answer contains two distinct conditions versus single-condition distractors, making the correct answer identifiable by length.
Three spot-the-error activities flagged for negative_stem — all false positives because the activity type inherently requires identifying incorrect content.
Two true/false questions (a7ece99d, 3ab80a24) flagged for negative_stem — both false positives; the stems are affirmative statements, not exclusion-format questions.
Explanations in true/false questions (a7ece99d, 776bd14f, 3ab80a24) restate the answer value without teaching the associated conditions or source context.
Flashcards are clean — all three have focused fronts, concise backs, and no scaffolding.

Paragraph-location scaffolding is the primary defect, affecting 9 of 9 questions and 3 of 6 activities. Stems open with 'Under 7-X-Y' and explanations cite 'Per 7-X-Y' — directing students to memorize paragraph numbers instead of operational rules.
Source fidelity is consistently accurate. All correct answers match the source blocks; distractors represent plausible operational errors or common misconceptions.

The automated negative_stem flag is a false positive on four items (ab5a854a, f778ae01, 483797dc, f3792c79). All four ask 'what should you do' or 'what must be told' — positive, direct questions with no exclusion or negation.

Flashcards are well-constructed. Each has a clear front-side prompt and a back that captures the retrieval target without overload. The 7-5-8 flashcard appropriately combines three interrelated procedural branches under one prompt.
One question (f778ae01) has a thin explanation that only restates the correct answer without teaching the operational principle present in the source blocks.

Distractor quality is good across all multiple-choice items. Incorrect choices represent genuine operational errors (e.g., issuing clearance automatically, applying IFR rules to VFR, denying vectoring altogether) rather than implausible nonsense.

Paragraph-location scaffolding is pervasive — stems begin with 'Under 7-6-X' and explanations cite 'Per 7-6-X' rather than stating the operational rule. This teaches students to navigate by paragraph number rather than by operational context.

Answer-length cues are present in 5 of 12 questions and 1 of 8 activities — the correct answer is conspicuously longer or shorter than the distractors, making the answer identifiable without content knowledge.

Three items across three formats (question af3b65fc, activity d5f3357d, flashcard 06c5cc5a) test the same termination-distance concept from 7-6-11. The activity is a near-duplicate of the question with only minor distractor variation.

Explanations frequently restate the correct answer text rather than teaching the controlling operational principle, exception, or condition. The 'Per 7-6-X' pattern replaces substantive teaching with a source citation.
Flashcard d4fc76b2 (7-6-2) has a front/back misalignment: the front asks when arrivals are assumed to want service, but the back answers with the controller's obligation to provide it.
True/false questions (afb35bd0, d7b0fd08, e4b32bd4, 239db3d5) quote or nearly quote source text, testing recognition rather than operational understanding.
Pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding in stems, explanations, and para_context fields across all three paragraphs — items teach 'where in the book' instead of the operational rule.
Over-generation from single-sentence sources: para 7-6-4 produced three questions and two activities from one body sentence, creating redundant multiple-choice equivalents.
True/false items frequently carry thin explanations that merely restate the answer without teaching the controlling principle.
Knowledge-check activities with multiple-choice choices duplicate existing MC questions in the same retrieval mode.
Distractor quality is consistently high — alternatives are operationally plausible and require understanding to distinguish.
Flashcards are well-formed with clear front prompts and concise backs; none required intervention.
Paragraph-location scaffolding is the primary defect — stems, para_context fields, and explanations consistently embed paragraph numbers instead of standing on their own operational footing.
True-false items for distance thresholds and prohibitions test recognition of a verbatim statement rather than application of the rule to operational conditions.
Situation-action correct answers tend to be longer than distractors, creating length-based cues.
Explanations for readback_check and spot_the_error activities use paragraph references rather than teaching the phraseology or operational principle directly.
Knowledge_check activities (4a148798, 7d963492) are the strongest items — self-contained stems, plausible distractors, operational explanations, no paragraph scaffolding.
Paragraph-location scaffolding: 12 of 12 questions and 3 of 7 activities embed paragraph numbers in stems, para_context, or explanations.
Answer-length cues: 3 multiple-choice items reveal the correct answer through systematically longer choice text.
Single-sentence item bloat: paragraphs 7-7-2 and 7-7-4 each have 3 questions testing the same fact from minor angle variations.
Thin explanations: 5 items use explanations that restate the answer with 'Per 7-7-X' rather than teaching the operational rule, condition, or exception.
Hinting language: 2 items use 'still' or concessive framing that telegraphs the correct answer.
Clean flashcards: all 4 flashcards are well-scoped, balanced, and free of defects.
Paragraph-location scaffolding: 7 of 8 questions and 4 of 8 activities embed paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, or para_context fields, framing items as paragraph-reference drills.

Thin explanations: 5 items (questions 8c51cdc3, 2b71b968; activities ca96ea1e, 9e78e111, 7124a341) use explanations that paraphrase the answer or cite a paragraph number without teaching the operational rule.

Single-sentence item bloat: paragraph 7-7-6 ('The tower must specify the approach interval') generates 2 questions, 2 activities, and 1 flashcard — all testing the same fact from minor angle variations.
Clean flashcards: all 3 flashcards are concise, well-scoped, bidirectional where appropriate, and free of defects.

False positive negative_stem flags: 3 items (985d0d78, 9e78e111, 7124a341) are flagged for negative stems but the instruction phrasing is intrinsic to their activity type (situation_action 'What should you do?' and spot_the_error 'One word is incorrect').
Readback check activity b736efd5 provides well-constructed phraseology practice with plausible distractor variations on the prescribed 'RESUME APPROPRIATE VFR ALTITUDES.'
Paragraph-location scaffolding in stems and explanations affecting 11 of 19 items
Thin or location-only explanations in 8 activities
Verbatim true/false questions testing quote recognition in 2 items
Strong situation-action activities with operational scenarios
Cross-format reinforcement for holding rule and exception mechanisms
Paragraph-location scaffolding in stems, explanations, and para_context fields affecting 14 of 22 items
Thin or paragraph-location-only explanations in 5 items
Two redundant true/false questions that duplicate content tested by other items
Answer-length cue revealing the correct answer in one multiple-choice question
Strong flashcard quality across all three cards with clean front/back, no scaffolding
Well-constructed situation_action and spot_the_error activities testing operational decision-making
Near-duplicate retrieval between question c755dc7b and activity 871e642d testing the same three separation methods via identical multiple-choice mode

Pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: 13 of the 14 items replaced opened stems or explanations with paragraph references such as 'Under 7-8-6' or 'Per 7-8-7a' instead of teaching the operational concept directly.

Thin explanations: Several true-false and requirement-check items explained only that a statement 'appears in' a paragraph rather than contrasting the true and false cases or teaching the operational distinction.
Answer-length cue: One knowledge-check activity combined both exception pairings from 7-8-6 into a single correct answer, creating a visible length difference from its shorter distractors.
Clean flashcards: All three flashcards are well-constructed — no paragraph scaffolding, appropriate back length, and correct retrieval targets for their card types.
Clean scenario-based items: The situation-action and readback-check activities in 7-8-8 are self-contained, use plausible distractors, and test operational application.
Cross-format coverage: The shard covers all three paragraphs across questions, activities, and flashcards, though within-paragraph 7-8-6 and 7-8-7 lack flashcards for individual sub-rules.

Pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: All ten questions open stems with 'Under 7-9-X' and almost all explanations reference 'Per 7-9-Xa/b/c' instead of teaching the rule directly. Four of seven activities embed paragraph references in para_context or explanations.

Thin explanation: One true-false question (e57061b6) explains only 'That guidance appears in the note to 7-9-2' — a pure document-location citation with zero operational teaching about why VFR/IFR segregation matters during radar outages.

Answer-length cue: The situation-action activity for 7-9-3 (26aa9349) has a correct answer roughly twice the word count of its distractors because it addresses both routing and spacing duties in one choice.
Clean flashcards: All three flashcards are well-constructed — no paragraph scaffolding, appropriate back length, and correct retrieval targets for their card types (definition and procedure).
Well-structured operational activities: The readback-check (cf762725) and spot-the-error (eab89a19) activities use real phraseology and plausible foil responses that test applied knowledge.

Within-paragraph reinforcement gaps: Paragraph 7-9-1 has only one flashcard covering equipment requirements but none covering where Class B services apply. Paragraph 7-9-3 lacks activity-level coverage of the controlled-airport departure service-equivalency rule (7-9-3c).
Paragraph-location scaffolding is the dominant defect across 10 of 18 items; stems open with 'Under 7-9-X' and explanations cite paragraph numbers instead of teaching the operational rule.
True/false items (9de15410, 4c0ed902, 98198da6, faee010c) consistently have one-line explanations that merely state where a requirement appears, offering no instructional value.
Two T/F items (98198da6, faee010c) are retrieval duplicates of multiple-choice questions testing the same fact in the same paragraph.
Answer-length cues appear where the correct choice is notably longer than distractors, especially in situation_action activities (c433e5f1, 82d19822).
One source-fidelity error: defa4b6a references 'paragraph 5-1-24' but the source block for 7-9-5b states 'paragraph 5-1-4'.
Flashcards are consistently well-formed with clear fronts, concise backs, and no scaffolding issues.

Paragraph-location scaffolding: every question stem and most activity para_context fields open with 'Under 7-9-X', and many explanations cite 'Per 7-9-X'. This teaches the document structure instead of the operational rule.
Thin explanations: several explanations restate the answer verbatim without teaching the operational condition or when the rule applies.
Single-sentence source over-generation: 7-9-8 is one sentence yet carries two questions (MC and T/F) plus two activities and a flashcard. The T/F question is a near-duplicate of the MC question.
Unsupported cross-reference: one activity explanation invents a TRSA comparison not present in the packet's source blocks for 7-9-8.
Spot-the-error explanation is thin: it identifies the correct word but does not teach when the phraseology is used.
generation_guidance: Write all question stems and explanations as self-contained operational prompts. Never prefix a stem with a paragraph, section, or chapter reference. Replace 'Per X-X-X' in explanations with the controlling rule stated directly.

Balance multiple-choice options so the correct answer does not stand out by length, specificity, or detail density. When the correct answer enumerates multiple conditions, give distractors comparable structure and length.

Do not test phraseology classification ('is this phraseology in paragraph X?'). Instead test operational use: when to issue the phraseology, what it triggers, what it must be paired with, and what the pilot must do in response.
For spot-the-error activities, keep the explanation focused on the operational impact of the error. For situation-action activities, strip paragraph-location language from the prompt context field.
Flashcards in this shard are already at good quality. Maintain the pattern: front identifies a single concept being recalled, back delivers concise retrieval content without paragraph citations.
Remove all paragraph-location scaffolding from stems, explanations, activity context fields, and flashcard text. Reference operational concepts directly without 'Under 7-X-Y' or 'Per 7-X-Y' prefixes.

When generating activity explanations, teach the controlling operational principle rather than pointing to a paragraph location. An explanation should state the rule, condition, or procedure — not where to find it.
Balance multiple-choice answer lengths so the correct answer does not stand out by word count. Elaborate distractors with plausible operational detail to match the specificity of the correct answer.
Keep flashcards to a single retrieval target per card. When a single card front asks multiple questions or the back covers distinct responsibilities (pilot vs. ATC), split into focused cards.
Verify source wording for exact terminology: 'not applied' is the correct phrasing for IFR separation under VFR-on-top, not 'suspended' or other synonyms.
Spot-the-error activities work well for prescribed phraseology. Ensure the explanation identifies why the correct phraseology matters operationally, not just which paragraph it comes from.

The readback_check, requirement_check, scope_check, knowledge_check, and situation_action activity formats are appropriately matched to their learning objectives in this shard. Continue pairing activity type with the skill being tested.
Never embed paragraph numbers, section references, or 'Under 7-X-Y' preambles in question stems, explanations, activity content, or flashcard text. State the operational rule directly.

Avoid circular questions where the stem contains the answer. If the correct choice is a verbatim restatement of the stem, restructure the question to test a distinct operational decision or condition.

When a paragraph contains multiple independent conditions (e.g., CVFP requirements, visual approach separation criteria for different runway spacing), ensure each question targets a specific condition with clear scope. Do not merge distinct scenarios into one answer set.

For true/false items, keep the statement concise and ensure the truth value is unambiguous from the source material. Long compound statements are harder for learners to parse and should be restructured as multiple-choice questions.
Readback check activities should explain why the correct readback matches the clearance, not merely cite a paragraph. Connect the readback to the operational procedure it fulfills.
Spot-the-error activities need explanations that contrast the error with the correct phraseology and explain the operational difference between the two, not simply identify which word is wrong.

Continue producing flashcards that are self-contained, with fronts that identify the concept being recalled and backs limited to one retrieval target. The three cards in this shard are a model for future generation.

Never prefix question stems with paragraph numbers (e.g., 'Under 7-4-6'). Write self-contained stems that describe the operational situation or ask the rule directly. If the source paragraph identity is needed for reference, place it only in metadata, never in the stem or explanation.

The contact approach paragraph (7-4-7) has several untested conditions — the vertical separation rule (at or below an altitude 1,000 ft below IFR traffic, not below 14 CFR 91.119 minima), the requirement for a published and functioning instrument approach, and the alternative-clearance requirement when weather makes the contact approach impracticable. Future passes should cover these.

The SVFR paragraph (7-5-1) has an untested subparagraph (c) — SVFR when the airport is reporting VFR but the pilot advises basic VFR cannot be maintained. This is a non-obvious operational scenario that deserves coverage.

The RVFP paragraph (7-4-6) has untested conditions: the waypoint join restriction (no joins at waypoints beginning or within an RF leg), the LOA provision for pilot request, and the requirement to specify the published RVFP name and landing runway in the clearance.

Continue using situation_action activities for operational decision-making. The pattern of describing a scenario where conditions are partially met and asking what the controller must do is effective.
Never embed paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, or para_context fields. Use operational condition language instead: 'When IFR traffic delays an SVFR clearance...' not 'Under 7-5-2...'
Balance answer choice lengths. A correct answer that is dramatically shorter or longer than every distractor telegraphs the answer regardless of content.

When the source rule includes multiple constraints (e.g., at-or-below altitude AND at least 500 ft below IFR AND not below MSA), every constraint must appear in the correct answer. Omission of a safety floor is a source-fidelity defect.
Explanations should teach the full controlling rule, not merely restate the answer. Include operational conditions, exceptions, and cross-references that a controller would need at the position.
This shard's flashcards are well-constructed — single retrieval targets, accurate front/back mappings, no overload. Use them as the model for future flashcard generation.

Cross-format reinforcement is adequate here. Future passes should ensure each essential source statement in 7-5-2, 7-5-3, and 7-5-4 has at least one question, one activity, and one flashcard covering it.
Do not embed paragraph or section references ('Under 7-5-5', 'Per 7-5-6') in question stems, activity prompts, or explanations. Write self-contained stems that describe the operational situation.
Balance answer choice lengths by making distractors similarly specific and detailed to the correct answer, not generic one-line dismissals.
For true/false items, write explanations that teach the operational conditions and thresholds, not merely the correct answer value.
Situation-action activities should embed the governing rule in the para_context field as a plain operational statement without paragraph-location prefixes.
Spot-the-error activities are inherently negative-framed; the negative_stem heuristic should not flag instructions like 'One word is incorrect. Tap it.'

The 7-5-7 paragraph has five distinct sub-procedures (a through e). The current items cover sub-procedures a, c, d, and e but not b (arriving aircraft operating outside the surface area). Future generation should cover the missed sub-procedure.
Strip all 'Under 7-X-Y' and 'According to paragraph…' phrasing from question stems. Rewrite as self-contained operational descriptions that test the rule, not the paragraph number.
Remove all 'Per 7-X-Y' citations from explanations. State the controlling rule directly without requiring paragraph-number knowledge.

When source blocks provide operational rationale (e.g., 'The pilot is best suited to determine the action to be taken since pilots operating under SVFR between sunrise and sunset are not required to be instrument rated'), incorporate that rationale into explanations rather than merely restating the correct answer.

Activity para_context fields should describe applicable rules without paragraph-number scaffolding. Cross-references to related procedures should use descriptive names (e.g., 'approach control service procedures') rather than paragraph numbers.

Situation_action activities in this shard are well-executed with realistic operational scenarios. Continue this pattern — provide enough situational detail to require the student to classify the case before selecting the action.

The false-positive negative_stem flagging (four items marked as negative that are straightforward positive questions) suggests the automated heuristic is overly broad. Do not react to this flag on direct 'what should you do' stems.
Never embed paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, or context fields. State the operational condition or situation directly — e.g. 'When the tower is closed' not 'Under 7-6-12.'
For multiple-choice items, ensure the correct answer and distractors are similar in length, specificity, and construction to avoid surface-level cues.
Avoid testing the same concept across more than two formats within a single paragraph. When a paragraph supports multiple distinct operational points, distribute coverage across formats accordingly.

For true/false items, test operational application (e.g. 'A controller may assign a landing sequence when the tower is closed — true or false?') rather than source-verification ('The source says X — is this true?').

Write explanations that state the controlling rule, condition, or exception in operational terms. Avoid 'Per 7-6-X, the answer is...' — instead teach what the controller must do and under what circumstances.
Flashcard fronts and backs must match in scope: if the front asks about a service assumption, the back should state what is assumed, not what the controller must provide.
Preserve phraseology-sensitive activity types (readback_check, spot_the_error) where exact wording matters, and continue pairing them with operationally plausible distractors.
When generating situation_action activities, keep para_context minimal and free of paragraph references — it should summarize only the relevant operational rule for the specific situation.
Strip all paragraph-number references from stems, explanations, and para_context fields. Replace 'Under 7-6-X' with operational phrasing that describes the rule context directly.
Limit item generation to at most two items per distinct source concept per retrieval mode. A single-sentence source should not produce more than one multiple-choice item.
Distinguish knowledge-check activities from MC questions by using a genuinely different retrieval task — do not wrap the same multiple-choice stem as both a question and a knowledge-check activity.
Maintain the distractor construction quality seen in this shard; distractors should remain operationally plausible misapplications of the rule.
Situation-action activities should preserve realistic scenario framing as demonstrated here; only the para_context field needs cleaning.
True/false items warrant fuller explanations that quote or closely paraphrase the controlling source statement rather than offering a one-line restatement.
Flashcard generation in this shard is a model of good practice: single retrieval target, operational phrasing, no paragraph references.
Never embed paragraph numbers in stems, para_context fields, or explanations. State the operational rule directly.
Prefer multiple-choice over true-false for operational rules unless the rule's binary nature (permitted/prohibited) is the central discrimination.
Match answer length to distractor length in situation-action items; trim correct answers to their essential operational component or expand distractors to comparable detail.
When a paragraph contains only one sentence (7-6-9), limit to at most two items in any single format to avoid mechanistic repetition.
For flashcards, split compound procedural steps into single-target cards unless the complete sequence is the required retrieval target.
Readback_check and spot_the_error explanations should reference the governing phraseology standard and the operational context, not paragraph numbers.
Keep knowledge_check activities as the preferred activity format — they produced the cleanest items in this shard.
Do not embed paragraph numbers in stems, answer choices, or explanations. Cite source blocks in item metadata only, not in student-facing text.
Balance answer choice lengths: a correct choice that is systematically longer or shorter than all distractors creates an unintended cue.
For single-sentence source paragraphs, produce at most one question and one flashcard. Use activity formats (knowledge_check or situation_action) for additional coverage if the concept warrants it.
Explanations must teach the operational rule, condition, or exception — not merely repeat the answer with a paragraph citation.
Avoid hint language: words like 'still,' 'even though,' and concessive clause framing narrow the student's reasoning to the correct answer before evaluating choices.
When a source rule contains an explicit exception (e.g., 'except when ISR displayed'), test the exception but balance the construction so the correct answer is not telegraphed by length or phrasing.
Do not embed paragraph numbers in stems, answer choices, explanations, or para_context fields. Source citations belong in item metadata, not student-facing text.

For single-sentence source paragraphs, produce at most one question and one flashcard. Reserve activities for operational application — a knowledge_check that mirrors an existing multiple_choice question duplicates retrieval mode.

Explanations must teach the controlling operational rule, condition, or exception — not restate the answer or cite the paragraph number. Connect the required action to the trigger condition (when it applies, what changes, who is responsible).
Phraseology items (readback_check, spot_the_error) should explain both the prescribed wording and the operational context in which it is used.
When a paragraph note establishes a default assumption (e.g., TRSA service is assumed unless declined), test the assumption operationally rather than asking 'what is stated in the note.'
Balance distraction plausibility across all choice items — avoid distractors that reference obviously unrelated services or facilities.
Never embed paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, or para_context fields
True/false items should test operational correctness of plausible-but-flawed actions, not restate source text
Explanations must teach the controlling rule, not name the paragraph location
Flashcards should target one specific retrieval item per card
When a rule has a norm and exception, test recognition of both parts in operational context
Never embed paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, or para_context fields; operational context is self-contained
True/false items should test recognition of an operational action's correctness, not verbatim restatement of source text
Explanations must teach the controlling rule, condition, or exception — never name only the paragraph location
Balance distractor length and specificity so the correct answer is not identifiable by its level of detail alone
When a concept is already tested by a question, the corresponding activity should exercise a different retrieval mode or application context
Spot-the-error activities are not negative stems; the automated negative_stem flag is a false positive for this format
Directive_check activities should tie the explanation to the operational requirement, not the paragraph number

Never embed paragraph numbers in stems, question text, activity prompts, or explanations. Present each rule as a self-contained operational concept — e.g., 'When a Class C separation exception is applied' not 'Under 7-8-6'.
For true-false and requirement-check items, write explanations that contrast the true and false cases or explain the operational distinction rather than citing paragraph location.

When a correct answer naturally combines two sub-rules (such as both exception categories in 7-8-6), ensure all distractors are of comparable length and grammatical complexity to avoid answer-length cues. Alternatively, test each sub-rule in a separate item.

Pair each major sub-rule with at least one question, one activity, and one flashcard to strengthen cross-format reinforcement. Currently 7-8-6a (helicopter/powered-lift exception) lacks a dedicated flashcard, and 7-8-7a (controlled-airport departures) lacks a dedicated flashcard.
For fill-in-the-blank phraseology items, introduce the context operationally (e.g., 'Complete the standard termination phraseology for Class C service') rather than by paragraph number.
Maintain the pattern seen in this shard's readback-check and situation-action activities — operational scenarios with plausible distractor actions drawn from common controller mistakes.

Never embed paragraph numbers in stems, question text, activity prompts, para_context, or explanations. Present each rule as a self-contained operational concept — e.g., 'What equipment is required for Class B operations' not 'Under 7-9-1, what equipment is required.'

For true-false items, write explanations that contrast the true and false cases or explain the operational distinction. Never explain an item by citing its paragraph location — teach the controlling rule instead.

When a situation-action correct answer naturally combines multiple sub-rules (routing preference plus spacing notification), either test each sub-rule in a separate item or ensure all distractors are of comparable length and grammatical complexity to avoid answer-length cues.

Pair each major sub-rule with at least one question, one activity, and one flashcard for cross-format reinforcement. Currently 7-9-1a (where Class B services apply) lacks a dedicated activity, 7-9-1b (equipment exceptions via 91.215) lacks dedicated flashcard coverage of the exception clause, and 7-9-3c (controlled-airport departure service parity) lacks activity-level coverage.
For readback-check and spot-the-error activities, continue using real phraseology and plausible foil responses drawn from common controller mistakes. The existing pattern in this shard is strong.

When summarizing source requirements in explanations, include the regulatory cross-reference (e.g., 14 CFR 91.215) when the source itself cites it, as this helps controllers understand the regulatory basis of their procedures.
Do not lead stems with 'Under 7-9-X' or embed paragraph numbers in question text. State the operational condition or scenario instead.
Explanations must teach the controlling rule, condition, or exception — not merely identify which paragraph or sub-paragraph contains it.
Avoid true/false items for operational rules that can support a plausible multiple-choice stem with operational distractors; T/F format invites thin explanations.

Do not create a true/false item and a multiple-choice item that test the same fact from the same paragraph; cross-format reinforcement should use different retrieval modes, not the same prompt in two formats.
Balance choice length: when the correct answer is a compound phrase, expand distractors to comparable length to avoid answer-length cues.
For situation_action activities, keep para_context high-level and contextual; do not restate the specific rules that answer the scenario question.
When citing cross-referenced paragraph numbers in explanations or choices, verify them against the source blocks; fabricated or typo'd references (e.g. 5-1-24 for 5-1-4) are source-fidelity errors.
Remove all paragraph-number references from question stems, explanations, and activity para_context fields. The student does not need to know the paragraph coordinate to apply the rule.

Do not generate more than one well-formed retrieval item per distinct fact when the source paragraph is a single sentence. A thin source supports at most one question or one activity plus at most one flashcard.
Do not reference sibling paragraphs (e.g., TRSA provisions) in explanations unless the cross-reference is present in the source blocks supplied in the packet.
When an activity has a correct phraseology token (spot_the_error), the explanation should teach when and why the controller issues that phraseology, not merely restate the correct word.
For readback_check activities, verify that every distractor is a plausible operational readback error, not an arbitrary phrase. The existing distractor set in this shard is good.

Flashcard card_type should match the retrieval target. 'definition' cards should recall a term or fact; 'procedure' cards should recall a sequence, condition, or obligation. The two flashcards in this shard are correctly typed.

shard_overviews: Shard 1 of chapter 7 covers five paragraphs (7-1-1 through 7-2-1) establishing Class A restrictions, VFR conditions, VFR arrival services, visual holding, and visual separation. The item set is factually accurate against source blocks, but suffers from pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: every question stem opens with 'Under X-X-X' and nearly every explanation cites a paragraph number. Two questions exhibit answer-length cues where the correct choice is substantially longer than all distractors. One true/false question reduces operational phraseology to a shallow classification exercise. All three activities have paragraph-location references in their explanations or prompt content. Four of five flashcards are clean and need no intervention. No items are factually wrong, redundant, or educationally harmful beyond the scaffolding pattern.

The shard contains substantively correct items across four paragraphs covering VFR-on-top clearances, cruising altitude advisories, visual approach definitions and go-around handling, and visual approach vector weather minima. Every item tests an operational concept from the source material. No items are factually wrong or educationally harmful in their core content. However, pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding undermines nearly every item: stems, explanations, and activity context fields repeatedly use 'Under 7-X-Y' and 'Per 7-X-Y' prefixes that teach document trivia instead of standalone operational knowledge. Activity explanations in 7-4-1 are especially thin, reducing to bare paragraph pointers. One flashcard inaccurately states IFR separation is 'suspended' rather than 'not applied,' and one question has a pronounced answer-length cue where the correct choice is substantially longer than all distractors.

The items across paragraphs 7-4-3, 7-4-4, and 7-4-5 are fundamentally accurate in their operational content but suffer from a pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding defect: nearly every item references 'Under 7-4-X' or 'Per 7-4-X' in its stem, explanation, or contextual fields. One question (cd15393a) is educationally hollow because the correct answer restates the stem verbatim. Another question (8d1a4408) merges two distinct operational scenarios into a contradictory answer. All three flashcards are clean and require no intervention. Two activities (37ac9ce1, 8cbfa20d) are well-constructed with no defects.

This shard covers three paragraphs (7-4-6 RVFP, 7-4-7 Contact Approach, 7-5-1 SVFR Authorization) with 22 items. The activities and flashcards are well-constructed — situation_action scenarios are operationally realistic, spot_the_error items target meaningful phraseology distinctions, and the readback_check for SVFR is a strong format fit. Content fidelity to source blocks is solid across all items. However, every quiz question (9 of 9) embeds 'Under 7-X-X' in the stem, substituting paragraph-location trivia for self-contained operational context. This was the only recurring defect; no items required removal or splitting.

This shard covers three well-scoped paragraphs (7-5-2 Priority, 7-5-3 Separation, 7-5-4 Altitude Assignment) with generally accurate content. All 18 items are factually correct and no item teaches an unsafe operational action. The dominant defect is paragraph-location scaffolding: 13 of 18 items embed 'Under 7-5-X' in stems or 'Per 7-5-X' in explanations, which teaches document-structure trivia instead of operational substance. One additional item (26624a85) omits the MSA floor constraint from its correct answer, and one question (0b752578) has a 3-word correct answer against 10–14-word distractors — an answer-length cue. The three flashcards are clean. Cross-format coverage is reasonable: priority rules, separation methods, and altitude assignment each appear in questions, activities, and flashcards.

This shard covering paragraphs 7-5-5 (Local Operations), 7-5-6 (Climb to VFR), and 7-5-7 (Ground Visibility Below 1 Mile) is substantively accurate with no source-fidelity errors. All correct answers align with the source blocks. The sole pervasive defect is paragraph-location scaffolding: 63 of 63 content fields across 16 replaceable items embed 'Under 7-5-X' or 'Per 7-5-X' references that teach paragraph trivia instead of operational substance. One question (6a52ac29) additionally exhibits a length cue where the correct answer is substantially longer than the three distractors. The three flashcards, two knowledge-check activities, and one multiple-choice activity are free of defects. The remaining 16 items require scaffolding removal and, in one case, answer-length balancing.

This shard covers three paragraphs (7-5-8, 7-6-1, 7-6-10) across 18 items. Source fidelity is uniformly strong: every item correctly reflects its controlling source statements with plausible distractors. The dominant defect is pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding — 12 of 18 items embed paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, or activity context fields, teaching document-trivia rather than operational substance. One question also carries a thin explanation that merely restates the answer without teaching the controlling principle. Three flashcards are well-formed with appropriate retrieval targets and need no intervention. Three activities (one knowledge_check and two situation_action) escaped paragraph scaffolding and are educationally sound as-is.

This shard covers four paragraphs (7-6-2, 7-6-3, 7-6-11, 7-6-12) on terminal radar service: service availability defaults, initial-contact sequencing, termination procedure and phraseology, and tower-inoperative services. The dominant defect is paragraph-location scaffolding — 17 of 24 items embed paragraph references in stems, explanations, or context fields, teaching document navigation instead of operational substance. All 12 questions carry paragraph_location_scaffold. A secondary pattern is answer-length cues in 5 items where the correct choice is notably longer or shorter than distractors. Three items (af3b65fc, d5f3357d, and flashcard 06c5cc5a) redundantly test the same termination-distance concept across question, activity, and flashcard formats. Explanations across the shard tend to restate the answer without connecting to the operational principle, though none invent unsupported safety rationales. Strengths: the readback_check (ab49c4da) and spot_the_error (1666e618) activities in 7-6-11 use appropriate activity types for phraseology; the situation_action activities in 7-6-12 (16da1577), 7-6-2 (2b77353d), and 7-6-3 (4f74b980) present operationally plausible scenarios with good distractors; and flashcards are generally concise with clean front/back pairs.

The shard covers three short paragraphs (7-6-4 through 7-6-6) on VFR arrival sequencing: identification prerequisites, holding procedures, and landing-sequence assignment authority. Source blocks are procedurally clear but very compact — each paragraph contains only one body sentence (and one note for 7-6-6), which limits the number of distinct learning targets. Source fidelity within items is intact; all answers are correct relative to the source. The principal quality failure is pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: nearly every item stem, explanation, and para_context field begins with 'Under 7-6-X' or 'Per 7-6-X', converting operational recall into document-structure trivia. A secondary failure is item redundancy: three or four items were generated from a single-sentence source using the same multiple-choice retrieval mode. Distractors across the shard are well-constructed — they represent plausible operational mistakes rather than arbitrary alternatives. Situation-action scenarios set up realistic decision points. All three flashcards are well-formed and ready to use.

Two-way radio communications and an operational transponder are normally required within Class C airspace, but operations without radio communications or transponder can be conducted by LOA, facility directive, or special arrangement with the controlling facility.

Allow the aircraft only in the outer area because special arrangements can never apply inside designated Class C airspace.

content.explanation: Radio and transponder equipment are normally required, but operations without them may be conducted by LOA, facility directive, or special arrangement with the Class C controlling facility. When an approved special arrangement exists, the operation should be allowed.

Limited radar coverage in one portion of the Class C area does not justify denial of Class C radar service in the entire area.

Hold all arriving and departing aircraft until the preferred radar sensor returns, because Class C aircraft should always be held during any outage.

content.explanation: Per 7-8-2 and its note, service continues to the extent coverage is sufficient, and limited coverage in one portion does not justify denying radar service throughout the entire Class C area.

choices.text: Keep the aircraft outside Bravo, approve or deny the request based on workload and traffic, and if the aircraft is being held give the pilot an expect-further-clearance advisory.
Allow the aircraft into Bravo without a clearance as long as the pilot remains VFR and watches for traffic.
Deny the request automatically because VFR aircraft may never be held outside Class B while awaiting further clearance.
Issue vectors inside Bravo first and decide whether to approve the request after the aircraft is already in the airspace.

content.explanation: VFR aircraft need an ATC clearance for Class B operations, and approval or denial must be based on workload, operational limitations, and traffic conditions. When VFR aircraft are held either inside or outside Bravo airspace, the controller must tell the pilot when to expect further clearance.

Do nothing further as long as the aircraft remains on a vector.

choices.text: Use the route that avoids VFR corridors and below-floor airspace when practical. After entry, keep the aircraft in Bravo. If spacing requires leaving, tell the pilot when leaving and reentering.
Prefer the VFR corridor because large turbine aircraft should use the same published routes as VFR traffic whenever possible for consistent traffic flow.

Keep the aircraft below the Bravo floor whenever possible because VFR corridors are reserved by ATC for sequencing turbine arrivals into the primary airport.

content.explanation: When ISR is displayed the 1 1/2-mile separation method is discontinued. The remaining separation options are 500 feet vertical separation and visual separation. Wake turbulence minima and cautionary advisories must also be applied when wake turbulence separation is required.
